problem_statement:
syntax highlighting is off after PR #99
Original comments - https://github.com/nushell/tree-sitter-nu/pull/99#issuecomment-2165419934

Notice how the `--` are different colors.

Copied from #99 
It looks like the highlighting is a bit off now. This is from the toolkit.nu file in the root of the nushell repo.

## Before
![image](https://github.com/nushell/tree-sitter-nu/assets/343840/ef2aafb8-f733-4032-b36f-93b2871ce603)
## After
![image](https://github.com/nushell/tree-sitter-nu/assets/343840/93a94e6e-9ad5-4753-983c-0f60b9ac2061)

Original comments - https://github.com/nushell/tree-sitter-nu/pull/99#issuecomment-2165419934

Notice how the `--` are different colors.

Copied from #99 
It looks like the highlighting is a bit off now. This is from the toolkit.nu file in the root of the nushell repo.

## Before
![image](https://github.com/nushell/tree-sitter-nu/assets/343840/ef2aafb8-f733-4032-b36f-93b2871ce603)
## After
![image](https://github.com/nushell/tree-sitter-nu/assets/343840/93a94e6e-9ad5-4753-983c-0f60b9ac2061)
test_patch:
diff --git a/test/corpus/decl/raw-strings.nu b/test/corpus/decl/raw-strings.nu
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..0d59d35
--- /dev/null
+++ b/test/corpus/decl/raw-strings.nu
@@ -0,0 +1,63 @@
+=====
+raw-strings-001-simple
+=====
+
+r#'string'#
+
+-----
+
+(nu_script
+  (pipeline
+    (pipe_element
+      (val_string
+          (raw_string_begin)
+          (raw_string_content)
+          (raw_string_end)))))
+
+=====
+raw-strings-002-simple
+=====
+
+r##'raw string: r#'bla'#'##
+
+-----
+
+(nu_script
+  (pipeline
+    (pipe_element
+      (val_string
+          (raw_string_begin)
+          (raw_string_content)
+          (raw_string_end)))))
+
+=====
+raw-strings-003-simple
+=====
+
+r#####'string'#####
+
+-----
+
+(nu_script
+  (pipeline
+    (pipe_element
+      (val_string
+          (raw_string_begin)
+          (raw_string_content)
+          (raw_string_end)))))
+
+=====
+raw-strings-004-with-ticks-and-tags
+=====
+
+r#####'string '#'##'#####
+
+-----
+
+(nu_script
+  (pipeline
+    (pipe_element
+      (val_string
+          (raw_string_begin)
+          (raw_string_content)
+          (raw_string_end)))))

problem_statement:
gtk-doc file names conflict with gtk-layer-shell
If there's going to be a separate repository for the gtk4 variant, then it would be nice if both gtk-layer-shell and gtk4-layer-shell could co-exist. Presently, the following files conflict:

* /usr/share/gtk-doc/html/gtk-layer-shell/api-index-full.html
* /usr/share/gtk-doc/html/gtk-layer-shell/gtk-layer-shell.devhelp2
* /usr/share/gtk-doc/html/gtk-layer-shell/home.png
* /usr/share/gtk-doc/html/gtk-layer-shell/index.html
* /usr/share/gtk-doc/html/gtk-layer-shell/left-insensitive.png
* /usr/share/gtk-doc/html/gtk-layer-shell/left.png
* /usr/share/gtk-doc/html/gtk-layer-shell/right-insensitive.png
* /usr/share/gtk-doc/html/gtk-layer-shell/right.png
* /usr/share/gtk-doc/html/gtk-layer-shell/style.css
* /usr/share/gtk-doc/html/gtk-layer-shell/up-insensitive.png
* /usr/share/gtk-doc/html/gtk-layer-shell/up.png

gtk-doc file names conflict with gtk-layer-shell

Support ruby 3.2 + 3.3+
This is still WIP, but I wanted to open it up so the general approach is clear. Main oddities for me right now:

- Having to free the data at the end of tracing. I guess that's ok? It was the only way I could figure out how to get the new `rb_internal_thread_specific_*` api working properly without a memory leak
- Name is not going to work correctly in ruby 3.3+ as this code is right now, since the events don't guarantee to fire from their associated thread. Ideally we'd get the name from the thread, but that is kind of tricky since ruby does not expose a method for it, and `Thread.list` doesn't contain any threads that have exited at the end of the tracing
- I know `object_id` is considered inefficient (https://github.com/ruby/ruby/pull/9276), but the overhead here seems minimal enough? I like it better as an identifier than a serial id and it lets MN threads work the same as native threads

### Commit notes

* There are lots of changes to how Ruby 3.3+ works with thread instrumentation.

* There is no guarantee which thread will trigger hooks. For instance, in Ruby 3.3 the RUBY_INTERNAL_THREAD_EVENT_STARTED event is always called from a different thread

* As a result, you have to use the `event_data->thread` member in conjunction with rb_internal_thread_specific_get/set to emulate "thread local" data tracking. You can't use actual thread locals anymore because the hooks are not fired on the same thread

* As a result, we can't reliably retrieve the native thread id because ruby does not expose a way to do this and using system calls won't work on different threads. Now uses object_id in ruby 3.3+

* These changes also work better with the M:N thread scheduler, since even if we could access native thread ids the M:N scheduler mostly reuses threads so the associated thread ids would be wrong

* Because the events can fire from different threads, the current way of getting the thread name (pthread_getname_np), will probably be wrong

* Using rb_thread_current() in the instrumentation hook calls causes a segfault, but it seems to work fine in the GC methods and when starting/stopping tracing

* Because we malloc the thread data in ruby 3.3, we free it up when we stop tracing, or whenever a thread exits
test_patch:
diff --git a/.github/workflows/test.yml b/.github/workflows/test.yml
index c41185b..ccf6da3 100644
--- a/.github/workflows/test.yml
+++ b/.github/workflows/test.yml
@@ -12,9 +12,12 @@ jobs:
       matrix:
         os: [ubuntu-latest, macos-latest, windows-latest]
         ruby: ['3.2', '3.3', head, debug]
+        mn_threads: ["0", "1"]
         exclude:
           - os: windows-latest
             ruby: debug
+          - ruby: "3.2"
+            mn_threads: "1"
     runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
     steps:
     - uses: actions/checkout@v2
@@ -22,4 +25,9 @@ jobs:
       with:
         ruby-version: ${{ matrix.ruby }}
         bundler-cache: true # runs 'bundle install' and caches installed gems automatically
-    - run: bundle exec rake
+    - name: bundle exec rake
+      run: |
+        ruby -v
+        bundle exec rake
+      env:
+        RUBY_MN_THREADS: ${{ matrix.mn_threads }}

- Name is not going to work correctly in ruby 3.3+ as this code is right now, since the events don't guarantee to fire from their associated thread. Ideally we'd get the name from the thread, but that is kind of tricky since ruby does not expose a method for it, and `Thread.list` doesn't contain any threads that have exited at the end of the tracing
- I know `object_id` is considered inefficient (https://github.com/ruby/ruby/pull/9276), but the overhead here seems minimal enough? I like it better as an identifier than a serial id and it lets MN threads work the same as native threads

### Commit notes

* There are lots of changes to how Ruby 3.3+ works with thread instrumentation.

* There is no guarantee which thread will trigger hooks. For instance, in Ruby 3.3 the RUBY_INTERNAL_THREAD_EVENT_STARTED event is always called from a different thread

* As a result, you have to use the `event_data->thread` member in conjunction with rb_internal_thread_specific_get/set to emulate "thread local" data tracking. You can't use actual thread locals anymore because the hooks are not fired on the same thread

* As a result, we can't reliably retrieve the native thread id because ruby does not expose a way to do this and using system calls won't work on different threads. Now uses object_id in ruby 3.3+

* These changes also work better with the M:N thread scheduler, since even if we could access native thread ids the M:N scheduler mostly reuses threads so the associated thread ids would be wrong

* Because the events can fire from different threads, the current way of getting the thread name (pthread_getname_np), will probably be wrong

* Using rb_thread_current() in the instrumentation hook calls causes a segfault, but it seems to work fine in the GC methods and when starting/stopping tracing

* Because we malloc the thread data in ruby 3.3, we free it up when we stop tracing, or whenever a thread exits

index c41185b..ccf6da3 100644
--- a/.github/workflows/test.yml
+++ b/.github/workflows/test.yml
@@ -12,9 +12,12 @@ jobs:
       matrix:
         os: [ubuntu-latest, macos-latest, windows-latest]
         ruby: ['3.2', '3.3', head, debug]
+        mn_threads: ["0", "1"]
         exclude:
           - os: windows-latest
             ruby: debug
+          - ruby: "3.2"
+            mn_threads: "1"
     runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
     steps:
     - uses: actions/checkout@v2
@@ -22,4 +25,9 @@ jobs:
       with:
         ruby-version: ${{ matrix.ruby }}
         bundler-cache: true # runs 'bundle install' and caches installed gems automatically
-    - run: bundle exec rake
+    - name: bundle exec rake
+      run: |
+        ruby -v
+        bundle exec rake
+      env:
+        RUBY_MN_THREADS: ${{ matrix.mn_threads }}

problem_statement:
Support ruby 3.2 + 3.3+
This is still WIP, but I wanted to open it up so the general approach is clear. Main oddities for me right now:

- Having to free the data at the end of tracing. I guess that's ok? It was the only way I could figure out how to get the new `rb_internal_thread_specific_*` api working properly without a memory leak
- Name is not going to work correctly in ruby 3.3+ as this code is right now, since the events don't guarantee to fire from their associated thread. Ideally we'd get the name from the thread, but that is kind of tricky since ruby does not expose a method for it, and `Thread.list` doesn't contain any threads that have exited at the end of the tracing
- I know `object_id` is considered inefficient (https://github.com/ruby/ruby/pull/9276), but the overhead here seems minimal enough? I like it better as an identifier than a serial id and it lets MN threads work the same as native threads

### Commit notes

* There are lots of changes to how Ruby 3.3+ works with thread instrumentation.

* There is no guarantee which thread will trigger hooks. For instance, in Ruby 3.3 the RUBY_INTERNAL_THREAD_EVENT_STARTED event is always called from a different thread

* As a result, you have to use the `event_data->thread` member in conjunction with rb_internal_thread_specific_get/set to emulate "thread local" data tracking. You can't use actual thread locals anymore because the hooks are not fired on the same thread

* As a result, we can't reliably retrieve the native thread id because ruby does not expose a way to do this and using system calls won't work on different threads. Now uses object_id in ruby 3.3+

* These changes also work better with the M:N thread scheduler, since even if we could access native thread ids the M:N scheduler mostly reuses threads so the associated thread ids would be wrong

* Because the events can fire from different threads, the current way of getting the thread name (pthread_getname_np), will probably be wrong

* Using rb_thread_current() in the instrumentation hook calls causes a segfault, but it seems to work fine in the GC methods and when starting/stopping tracing

* Because we malloc the thread data in ruby 3.3, we free it up when we stop tracing, or whenever a thread exits

problem_statement:
Support ruby 3.2 + 3.3+
This is still WIP, but I wanted to open it up so the general approach is clear. Main oddities for me right now:

- Having to free the data at the end of tracing. I guess that's ok? It was the only way I could figure out how to get the new `rb_internal_thread_specific_*` api working properly without a memory leak
- Name is not going to work correctly in ruby 3.3+ as this code is right now, since the events don't guarantee to fire from their associated thread. Ideally we'd get the name from the thread, but that is kind of tricky since ruby does not expose a method for it, and `Thread.list` doesn't contain any threads that have exited at the end of the tracing
- I know `object_id` is considered inefficient (https://github.com/ruby/ruby/pull/9276), but the overhead here seems minimal enough? I like it better as an identifier than a serial id and it lets MN threads work the same as native threads

### Commit notes

* There are lots of changes to how Ruby 3.3+ works with thread instrumentation.

* There is no guarantee which thread will trigger hooks. For instance, in Ruby 3.3 the RUBY_INTERNAL_THREAD_EVENT_STARTED event is always called from a different thread

* As a result, you have to use the `event_data->thread` member in conjunction with rb_internal_thread_specific_get/set to emulate "thread local" data tracking. You can't use actual thread locals anymore because the hooks are not fired on the same thread

* As a result, we can't reliably retrieve the native thread id because ruby does not expose a way to do this and using system calls won't work on different threads. Now uses object_id in ruby 3.3+

* These changes also work better with the M:N thread scheduler, since even if we could access native thread ids the M:N scheduler mostly reuses threads so the associated thread ids would be wrong

* Because the events can fire from different threads, the current way of getting the thread name (pthread_getname_np), will probably be wrong

* Using rb_thread_current() in the instrumentation hook calls causes a segfault, but it seems to work fine in the GC methods and when starting/stopping tracing

* Because we malloc the thread data in ruby 3.3, we free it up when we stop tracing, or whenever a thread exits

problem_statement:
Support ruby 3.2 + 3.3+
This is still WIP, but I wanted to open it up so the general approach is clear. Main oddities for me right now:

- Having to free the data at the end of tracing. I guess that's ok? It was the only way I could figure out how to get the new `rb_internal_thread_specific_*` api working properly without a memory leak
- Name is not going to work correctly in ruby 3.3+ as this code is right now, since the events don't guarantee to fire from their associated thread. Ideally we'd get the name from the thread, but that is kind of tricky since ruby does not expose a method for it, and `Thread.list` doesn't contain any threads that have exited at the end of the tracing
- I know `object_id` is considered inefficient (https://github.com/ruby/ruby/pull/9276), but the overhead here seems minimal enough? I like it better as an identifier than a serial id and it lets MN threads work the same as native threads

### Commit notes

* There are lots of changes to how Ruby 3.3+ works with thread instrumentation.

* There is no guarantee which thread will trigger hooks. For instance, in Ruby 3.3 the RUBY_INTERNAL_THREAD_EVENT_STARTED event is always called from a different thread

* As a result, you have to use the `event_data->thread` member in conjunction with rb_internal_thread_specific_get/set to emulate "thread local" data tracking. You can't use actual thread locals anymore because the hooks are not fired on the same thread

* As a result, we can't reliably retrieve the native thread id because ruby does not expose a way to do this and using system calls won't work on different threads. Now uses object_id in ruby 3.3+

* These changes also work better with the M:N thread scheduler, since even if we could access native thread ids the M:N scheduler mostly reuses threads so the associated thread ids would be wrong

* Because the events can fire from different threads, the current way of getting the thread name (pthread_getname_np), will probably be wrong

* Using rb_thread_current() in the instrumentation hook calls causes a segfault, but it seems to work fine in the GC methods and when starting/stopping tracing

* Because we malloc the thread data in ruby 3.3, we free it up when we stop tracing, or whenever a thread exits

- I know `object_id` is considered inefficient (https://github.com/ruby/ruby/pull/9276), but the overhead here seems minimal enough? I like it better as an identifier than a serial id and it lets MN threads work the same as native threads

* Because the events can fire from different threads, the current way of getting the thread name (pthread_getname_np), will probably be wrong

* Using rb_thread_current() in the instrumentation hook calls causes a segfault, but it seems to work fine in the GC methods and when starting/stopping tracing

* Because we malloc the thread data in ruby 3.3, we free it up when we stop tracing, or whenever a thread exits

Ideally we'd get the name from the thread, but that is kind of tricky since ruby does not expose a method for it, and `Thread.list` doesn't contain any threads that have exited at the end of the tracing
- I know `object_id` is considered inefficient (https://github.com/ruby/ruby/pull/9276), but the overhead here seems minimal enough? I like it better as an identifier than a serial id and it lets MN threads work the same as native threads

### Commit notes

* There are lots of changes to how Ruby 3.3+ works with thread instrumentation.

* There is no guarantee which thread will trigger hooks. For instance, in Ruby 3.3 the RUBY_INTERNAL_THREAD_EVENT_STARTED event is always called from a different thread

* As a result, you have to use the `event_data->thread` member in conjunction with rb_internal_thread_specific_get/set to emulate "thread local" data tracking. You can't use actual thread locals anymore because the hooks are not fired on the same thread

* As a result, we can't reliably retrieve the native thread id because ruby does not expose a way to do this and using system calls won't work on different threads. Now uses object_id in ruby 3.3+

* These changes also work better with the M:N thread scheduler, since even if we could access native thread ids the M:N scheduler mostly reuses threads so the associated thread ids would be wrong

* Because the events can fire from different threads, the current way of getting the thread name (pthread_getname_np), will probably be wrong

* Using rb_thread_current() in the instrumentation hook calls causes a segfault, but it seems to work fine in the GC methods and when starting/stopping tracing

* Because we malloc the thread data in ruby 3.3, we free it up when we stop tracing, or whenever a thread exits

+    - name: bundle exec rake
+      run: |
+        ruby -v
+        bundle exec rake
+      env:
+        RUBY_MN_THREADS: ${{ matrix.mn_threads }}

- Having to free the data at the end of tracing. I guess that's ok? It was the only way I could figure out how to get the new `rb_internal_thread_specific_*` api working properly without a memory leak
- Name is not going to work correctly in ruby 3.3+ as this code is right now, since the events don't guarantee to fire from their associated thread. Ideally we'd get the name from the thread, but that is kind of tricky since ruby does not expose a method for it, and `Thread.list` doesn't contain any threads that have exited at the end of the tracing
- I know `object_id` is considered inefficient (https://github.com/ruby/ruby/pull/9276), but the overhead here seems minimal enough? I like it better as an identifier than a serial id and it lets MN threads work the same as native threads

### Commit notes

* There are lots of changes to how Ruby 3.3+ works with thread instrumentation.

* There is no guarantee which thread will trigger hooks. For instance, in Ruby 3.3 the RUBY_INTERNAL_THREAD_EVENT_STARTED event is always called from a different thread

* As a result, you have to use the `event_data->thread` member in conjunction with rb_internal_thread_specific_get/set to emulate "thread local" data tracking. You can't use actual thread locals anymore because the hooks are not fired on the same thread

* As a result, we can't reliably retrieve the native thread id because ruby does not expose a way to do this and using system calls won't work on different threads. Now uses object_id in ruby 3.3+

* These changes also work better with the M:N thread scheduler, since even if we could access native thread ids the M:N scheduler mostly reuses threads so the associated thread ids would be wrong

* Because the events can fire from different threads, the current way of getting the thread name (pthread_getname_np), will probably be wrong

* Using rb_thread_current() in the instrumentation hook calls causes a segfault, but it seems to work fine in the GC methods and when starting/stopping tracing

* Because we malloc the thread data in ruby 3.3, we free it up when we stop tracing, or whenever a thread exits
test_patch:
diff --git a/.github/workflows/test.yml b/.github/workflows/test.yml
index c41185b..ccf6da3 100644
--- a/.github/workflows/test.yml
+++ b/.github/workflows/test.yml
@@ -12,9 +12,12 @@ jobs:
       matrix:
         os: [ubuntu-latest, macos-latest, windows-latest]
         ruby: ['3.2', '3.3', head, debug]
+        mn_threads: ["0", "1"]
         exclude:
           - os: windows-latest
             ruby: debug
+          - ruby: "3.2"
+            mn_threads: "1"
     runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
     steps:
     - uses: actions/checkout@v2
@@ -22,4 +25,9 @@ jobs:
       with:
         ruby-version: ${{ matrix.ruby }}
         bundler-cache: true # runs 'bundle install' and caches installed gems automatically
-    - run: bundle exec rake
+    - name: bundle exec rake
+      run: |
+        ruby -v
+        bundle exec rake
+      env:
+        RUBY_MN_THREADS: ${{ matrix.mn_threads }}

problem_statement:
Support ruby 3.2 + 3.3+
This is still WIP, but I wanted to open it up so the general approach is clear. Main oddities for me right now:

- Having to free the data at the end of tracing. I guess that's ok? It was the only way I could figure out how to get the new `rb_internal_thread_specific_*` api working properly without a memory leak
- Name is not going to work correctly in ruby 3.3+ as this code is right now, since the events don't guarantee to fire from their associated thread. Ideally we'd get the name from the thread, but that is kind of tricky since ruby does not expose a method for it, and `Thread.list` doesn't contain any threads that have exited at the end of the tracing
- I know `object_id` is considered inefficient (https://github.com/ruby/ruby/pull/9276), but the overhead here seems minimal enough? I like it better as an identifier than a serial id and it lets MN threads work the same as native threads

### Commit notes

* There are lots of changes to how Ruby 3.3+ works with thread instrumentation.

* There is no guarantee which thread will trigger hooks. For instance, in Ruby 3.3 the RUBY_INTERNAL_THREAD_EVENT_STARTED event is always called from a different thread

* As a result, you have to use the `event_data->thread` member in conjunction with rb_internal_thread_specific_get/set to emulate "thread local" data tracking. You can't use actual thread locals anymore because the hooks are not fired on the same thread

* As a result, we can't reliably retrieve the native thread id because ruby does not expose a way to do this and using system calls won't work on different threads. Now uses object_id in ruby 3.3+

* These changes also work better with the M:N thread scheduler, since even if we could access native thread ids the M:N scheduler mostly reuses threads so the associated thread ids would be wrong

* Because the events can fire from different threads, the current way of getting the thread name (pthread_getname_np), will probably be wrong

* Using rb_thread_current() in the instrumentation hook calls causes a segfault, but it seems to work fine in the GC methods and when starting/stopping tracing

* Because we malloc the thread data in ruby 3.3, we free it up when we stop tracing, or whenever a thread exits
test_patch:
diff --git a/.github/workflows/test.yml b/.github/workflows/test.yml
index c41185b..ccf6da3 100644
--- a/.github/workflows/test.yml
+++ b/.github/workflows/test.yml
@@ -12,9 +12,12 @@ jobs:
       matrix:
         os: [ubuntu-latest, macos-latest, windows-latest]
         ruby: ['3.2', '3.3', head, debug]
+        mn_threads: ["0", "1"]
         exclude:
           - os: windows-latest
             ruby: debug
+          - ruby: "3.2"
+            mn_threads: "1"
     runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
     steps:
     - uses: actions/checkout@v2
@@ -22,4 +25,9 @@ jobs:
       with:
         ruby-version: ${{ matrix.ruby }}
         bundler-cache: true # runs 'bundle install' and caches installed gems automatically
-    - run: bundle exec rake
+    - name: bundle exec rake
+      run: |
+        ruby -v
+        bundle exec rake
+      env:
+        RUBY_MN_THREADS: ${{ matrix.mn_threads }}
diff:
--- ext/gvl_tracing_native_extension/gvl_tracing.c
+++ ext/gvl_tracing_native_extension/gvl_tracing.c
@@ -204,7 +204,7 @@
     on_thread_event,
     (
       RUBY_INTERNAL_THREAD_EVENT_READY |
-      RUBY_INTERNAL_THREAD_EVENT_RESUMED |
+      RUBY_INTERNAL_THREAD_EVENT_STARTED |
       RUBY_INTERNAL_THREAD_EVENT_SUSPENDED |
       RUBY_INTERNAL_THREAD_EVENT_STARTED |
       RUBY_INTERNAL_THREAD_EVENT_EXITED
